title: Show HN: Getting GLM 5.2 running on my slow computer
titleZh: 展示 HN：让 GLM 5.2 在我的低配电脑上跑起来
author: vforno
relevanceReason: 命中 5 个 AI/科技关键词。
textRawHtml:
A few days ago I found myself trying out GLM 5.2 and was really positively impressed. The capabilities and security I was getting from this LLM are similar to those I've gotten from models like Claude or GPT, and this really surprised me.

But then I thought, "I wonder how it would work on a normal computer like mine," and above all, "I wonder if it would work without going into OOM on a computer like mine." So I started working with the help of agents to test this possibility.

I started converting the model to int4, understanding MTP usage, and if possible implementing DSA for long context. How it responds in int4 and whether the quality is maintained or not. Until I got to the point, on my computer with 32GB of RAM, I was able to communicate with GLM 5.2 with times that, of course, aren't high in cold start, but even then, we're talking about 0.1 tok/s, but that wasn't important to me. The important thing was the journey to reach this goal. I just wanted it to work at all costs, even slowly.

So I created Colibrì, which was born from a very simple idea, to be honest, but tested in every way, where a 744B Mixture-of-Experts model activates only ~40B parameters per token—and only ~11 GB of those change from token to token (the routed experts). So:

The dense part (attention, shared experts, embeddings—~17B params) stays resident in RAM at int4 (~9.9 GB); The 21,504 routed experts (75 MoE layers × 256 experts + the MTP head, ~19 MB each at int4) live on disk (~370 GB) and are streamed on demand, with a per-layer LRU cache, an optional pinned hot-store, and the OS page cache as a free L2.

The engine is a single C file (c/glm.c, ~1,300 lines) plus small headers. No BLAS, no Python at runtime, no GPU.No GPU or serious hardware because I don't have that hardware so I can't test it on hardware that is more powerful than my computer.Colibrì is a one-person project, written and tested entirely on a 12-core laptop with 25 GB of RAM — the numbers above are the ceiling of what I can measure at home.

Any feedback is welcome! (and if anyone wanted to participate in the project I would be delighted)

Repo: https://github.com/JustVugg/colibri
textZhHtml:
几天前，我试用了 GLM 5.2，结果真的让我眼前一亮。这款大模型展现出的能力和安全性，与我之前使用 Claude 或 GPT 等模型时的体验不相上下，这着实让我感到惊喜。

但转念一想，“不知道它在我这样的普通电脑上运行效果如何”，更重要的是，“不知道它在我这样的电脑上能不能顺利运行而不出现内存溢出”。于是，我开始借助智能体来测试这种可能性。

我开始将模型量化为int4，研究MTP的用法，并尽可能为长上下文实现DSA。观察它在int4精度下的响应表现，以及质量是否还能保持。直到最后，在我那台32GB内存的电脑上，我终于能和GLM 5.2进行交互了。冷启动时的响应时间自然算不上快，即便如此，生成速度也只有0.1 tok/s，但这对我来说并不重要。重要的是实现这一目标的整个过程。我只想不惜一切代价让它跑起来，哪怕慢一点也没关系。

所以我创建了Colibrì，说实话，它源于一个非常简单的想法，但经过了全方位的测试。其中744B的混合专家模型每个token仅激活约40B个参数——而且只有约11GB的参数会在token之间发生变化（即路由专家）。所以：

密集部分（注意力机制、共享专家、嵌入层——约170亿参数）以int4格式常驻内存（约9.9 GB）；21,504个路由专家（75个MoE层 × 256个专家 + MTP头，在int4格式下每个约19 MB）存储在磁盘上（约370 GB）并按需流式加载，配备逐层LRU缓存、可选的锁定热存储区，并将操作系统页缓存作为免费的L2缓存。

该引擎仅由一个 C 语言文件（c/glm.c，约 1,300 行）和几个小型头文件组成。没有 BLAS，运行时不需要 Python，也没有 GPU。没有 GPU 或高端硬件，因为我没有那些设备，所以无法在比我电脑更强大的硬件上进行测试。Colibrì 是一个单人项目，完全在一台配备 12 核处理器和 25 GB 内存的笔记本电脑上编写和测试——上述数据就是我在家里所能测得的极限。

欢迎提供任何反馈！（如果有人愿意参与这个项目，我将非常高兴）

仓库： https://github.com/JustVugg/colibri
summaryRaw: Tiny engine, immense model. Run GLM-5.2 (744B-parameter MoE) on a consumer machine with ~25 GB of RAM — in pure C, with zero dependencies, by streaming experts from disk. $ ./coli chat 🐦 colibrì v1.0 — GLM-5.2 · 744B MoE · int4 · streaming CPU ✓ ready in 32s · resident 9.9 GB › ciao! ◆ Ciao! 😊 Come posso aiutarti oggi?
The idea A 744B Mixture-of-Experts model activates only ~40B parameters per token — and only ~11 GB of those change from token to token (the routed experts). So:

the dense part (attention, shared experts, embeddings — ~17B params) stays resident in RAM at int4 (~9.9 GB); the 21,504 routed experts (75 MoE layers × 256 experts + the MTP head, ~19 MB each at int4) live on disk (~370 GB) and are streamed on demand, with a per-layer LRU cache, an optional pinned hot-store, and the OS page cache as a free L2.

The engine is a single C file (c/glm.c, ~2,400 lines) plus small headers. No BLAS, no Python at runtime, no GPU required (an opt-in CUDA tier for pinned experts exists — see below). What's implemented

Faithful GLM-5.2 (glm_moe_dsa) forward — validated token-exact against a transformers oracle (teacher-forcing 32/32, greedy 20/20 on a tiny-random model with the real architecture). MLA attention (q/kv-LoRA, interleaved partial RoPE) with compressed KV-cache: 576 floats/token instead of 32,768 (57× smaller — GLM-5.2 has 64 heads and no GQA). DeepSeek-V3-style sigmoid router (noaux_tc, routed_scaling_factor), shared expert, first-3-dense layers. Native MTP speculative decoding — GLM-5.2's own multi-token-prediction head (layer 78) drafts tokens that the main model verifies in one batched forward. The head must be int8 (the converter does this by default): at int4 draft acceptance collapses to 0–4% and speculation never engages; at int8 it's 39–59% acceptance, 2.2–2.8 tokens/forward (community-measured, #8). Lossless — and stays lossless under sampling via rejection sampling. Honest caveat from the same measurement: on a cold cache each verified draft routes to extra experts (~660 → ~1100 expert-loads/token), so speculation can be a net time loss until the cache/pin warms up — the adaptive guard and DRAFT=0 are there for that. Grammar-forced speculative drafts (GRAMMAR=file.gbnf, #48) — on constrained-output workloads (JSON/NDJSON, function calling, structured extraction) the grammar itself is a third draft source: wherever it admits exactly one legal byte (braces, quotes, key names, enum bodies), that forced span is tokenized and injected as pre-accepted drafts with ~1.0 acceptance — no draft head, no lookup table, and it engages even with the int4 MTP head from #8. It never constrains sampling: forced spans are verified in the same batch-union forward as any draft, so a wrong or out-of-sync grammar cannot change the output — worst case is rejected drafts, and an adaptive guard turns the source off below 50% acceptance. Byte-level GBNF subset (literals, char classes, | ( ) ? * +, comments); GRAMMAR_DRAFT=n caps the forced span per forward (default 24). Composes with DRAFT/MTP, which fill the free-text gaps between forced spans. True sampling — temperature + nucleus, defaults tuned for int4 reality (0.7 / 0.90; the official 1.0 / 0.95 samples quantization noise from the tail). Integer-dot kernels (Q8_0-style int8 activations, AVX2 maddubs): int8 matmuls 1.4–2.5× faster (119 GFLOP/s measured), int4 1.8× in batch — routing decided per shape by measurement (int4 single-row stays f32: it measured slower). MLA weight absorption (DeepSeek trick) for decode: no per-token k/v reconstruction — the query absorbs kv_b, context is projected after attention. Validated exact: TF 32/32 and generation 20/20 with absorption forced everywhere. Async expert readahead: while one block of experts is being multiplied, the kernel is already reading the next (WILLNEED). Quantization kernels: int8 / packed int4 / packed int2, per-row scales, AVX2, dequant-on-use. Packing validated bit-identical to the int8 container. DSA sparse attention — GLM-5.2's lightning indexer, faithful to the reference glm_moe_dsa modeling: per-layer top-2048 causal key selection (full/shared indexer layers), auto-detected from the out-idx-* weights (--indexer converter mode, ~189 MB extracted from the FP8 repo). Validated exact: forcing the selection to keep every key reproduces dense attention token-for-token. DSA=0 disables, DSA_TOPK overrides. KV-cache persistence — conversations reopen warm across engine restarts: serve mode appends the compressed MLA KV to .coli_kv after every turn (~182 KB/token, crash-safe) and resumes it at startup with zero re-prefill. Validated byte-identical to an uninterrupted session. KVSAVE=0 disables. Router-lookahead prefetch (PILOT=1, experimental) — the next layer's routing is 71.6% predictable from the current layer's post-attention state (measured); a dedicated I/O thread prefetches those experts while the current layer computes. Batch-union MoE: in prefill (and MTP verification), each unique expert of the batch is read once and applied to every position that routes to it. Byte-level BPE tokenizer in C (GPT-2-style with Unicode-property regex, 320k merges). RAM safety: the expert cache is auto-sized from MemAvailable at startup — an honest peak projection (working set, KV, MTP row, reconstruction buffers) so the kernel OOM-killer never fires. Offline FP8→int4 converter (c/tools/convert_fp8_to_int4.py): downloads one shard at a time (~5 GB), dequants (128×128 block scales), requantizes to the engine's container, deletes the shard — the 756 GB FP8 checkpoint never needs to exist on disk at once. Resumable.
summaryZh: Tiny engine, immense model. Run GLM-5.2 (744B-parameter MoE) on a consumer machine with ~25 GB of RAM — in pure C, with zero dependencies, by streaming experts from disk. $ ./coli chat 🐦 colibrì v1.0 — GLM-5.2 · 744B MoE · int4 · streaming CPU ✓ ready in 32s · resident 9.9 GB › ciao! ◆ Ciao! 😊 Come posso aiutarti oggi?
The idea A 744B Mixture-of-Experts model activates only ~40B parameters per token — and only ~11 GB of those change from token to token (the routed experts). So:

comments.author: lopatin
comments.textRawHtml:
After spending way too much time with Fable a few days ago, I noticed a new hallmark of AI generated text is using the word "honest" everywhere, in a somewhat self congratulating way.

Some things that give it away to me:

- "Honest numbers (WSL2, 12 cores, 25 GB RAM, NVMe via VHDX)"

- "an honest peak projection (working set, KV, MTP row, reconstruction buffers) so the kernel OOM-killer never fires."

- "Honest caveat from the same measurement: ..."

It's the new "It's not X, it's Y". I have no issue with this, I just found it amusing.

Cool project btw!
comments.textZhHtml:
几天前在《神鬼寓言》上花了太多时间后，我注意到人工智能生成的文本的一个新标志是到处使用“诚实”这个词，以某种自我祝贺的方式。

一些事情让我明白了：

-“诚实的数字（WSL2、12 核、25 GB RAM、通过 VHDX 的 NVMe）”

-“诚实的峰值投影（工作集、 KV、MTP 行、重建缓冲区），因此内核 OOM 杀手永远不会触发。”

-“来自同一测量的诚实警告：...”

这是新的“这不是 X，而是 Y”。我对此没有任何问题，我只是觉得它很有趣。

顺便说一句，很酷的项目！

children.author: abe94
children.textRawHtml:

from recent fable sessions:

children.textZhHtml:
我在最近的寓言会议中看到了 `real` 这个词的大量使用：

- 这个差距是真实的：3,873 个流

- 真正的转化过滤器类型是：

children.author: badatnames
children.textRawHtml: I have almost completely stopped writing real in my own output as a direct consequence of this. Maybe next year they'll catch up with my alternative wordings and I'll end up switching back
children.textZhHtml: 作为这一点的直接后果，我几乎完全停止在自己的输出中写出真实的内容。也许明年他们会赶上我的替代措辞，而我最终会切换回来

children.author: fibonachos
children.textRawHtml:
I’ve started learning into the various AI-isms in my prompts. Most recently, em dashes to chain related information in a single sentence.

“Something something main point — but also pay attention to this related thing — finish main point.”

Anecdotally, it seems to produce more consistent results when I “speak its language”.

On the output side, I’ve become much more aggressive about trimming and editing any prose it produces that I intend on sharing. Both for my own benefit to improve comprehension, but also because dumping the raw, verbose output on my colleagues feels lazy and disrespectful of their time.
children.textZhHtml:
我开始在提示词中研究各种“AI腔调”。最近在用破折号把相关信息串联在一个句子里。

“某某某主要观点——但也要注意这个相关事项——完成主要观点。”

就我个人经验而言，当我“说它的语言”时，似乎能产生更一致的结果。

在输出方面，对于我打算分享的AI生成的文本，我变得更加大刀阔斧地删减和编辑。这既是为了提高我自己的理解效率，也是因为把原始、冗长的输出直接甩给同事显得太敷衍，不尊重他们的时间。

children.author: taneq
children.textRawHtml:
So you’re code switching to incorporate AI-specific ‘vocal fry’ in order to better communicate with it?

That’s not just mimicry. It’s insightful. And it just might work. ;)
children.textZhHtml:
所以你在进行语码转换，融入AI特有的“机械腔调”，以便更好地与它交流？

这不仅仅是模仿。这很有见地。而且说不定真管用。;)

children.author: fibonachos
children.textRawHtml: What’s interesting is that it maps well to how my brain actually works. My chain of thought is very non-linear. Maybe even a little chaotic, going off on tangents before returning to the main thread as it were. This has always made written and verbal communication a bit of a challenge, but the AI seems to parse my unedited stream-of-consciousness without issue.
children.textZhHtml: 有趣的是，这与我大脑的实际运作方式非常契合。我的思维链条非常非线性。甚至可能有点混乱，总是先跑题，然后再回到主线。这一直让我在书面和口头表达上有些吃力，但AI似乎能毫无障碍地解析我未经编辑的意识流。

children.author: lukas9
children.textRawHtml: This is what I've noticed lately. I write articles and over the past year or two I had to stop using specific words or change my writing style a bit many times, because people kept asking if it was AI. It just makes me sad when I show someone my work and the first thing they say is "Is this AI"?
children.textZhHtml: 这也是我最近注意到的现象。我写文章，在过去的一两年里，我不得不多次停止使用某些特定词汇或稍微改变一下写作风格，因为人们总是问这是不是AI写的。当我向别人展示我的作品，而他们开口的第一句话却是“这是AI写的吗？”，这真的让我很难过。

children.author: arximboldi
children.textRawHtml: also substantivizing everything and using "gate", "latch", at every turn. a normal person would say "add the pasta once the water boils" or "the water should be boiling when you add the pasta". Claude would say: water boiling state gates the pasta addition. it sounds obnoxious and engineery but it's so mentally tiring to read and decypher.
children.textZhHtml: 还实体化一切，并在每一个回合使用“门”、“闩”。正常人会说“水沸腾后加入面食”或“加入面食时水应该沸腾”。克劳德会说：水沸腾的状态门控了面食的添加。这听起来很令人讨厌，而且很工程化，但阅读和破译它却让人精神疲惫。

children.author: brettpro
children.textRawHtml: It's been "mathematical" from Gemini for me. Mathematically verified yaml files for Home Assistant, mathematical confirmation of (incorrect) CLI args for a tool, mathematically indistinguishable nutrient profiles of two ingredients.
children.textZhHtml: 双子座对我来说是“数学”的。经过数学验证的家庭助理 yaml 文件，对工具的（错误）CLI 参数进行数学确认，两种成分的数学上无法区分的营养成分。

children.author: walrus01
children.textRawHtml: Unfortunately we're much too late for realnetworks and realplayer to be relevant anymore.
children.textZhHtml: 不幸的是，我们已经太晚了，realnetworks 和 realplayer 已经不再具有相关性了。

children.author: kzrdude
children.textRawHtml: It aligns pretty well with corporate and pr-speak, but the LLMs really weaponize it and tire out the phrases quickly. I bet this is interesting for linguists.
children.textZhHtml: 它与企业和公关用语非常吻合，但法学硕士确实将其武器化，并且很快就厌倦了这些短语。我敢打赌这对语言学家来说很有趣。

children.author: FeepingCreature
children.textRawHtml: I suspect it's a signature of anti-fabrication training.
children.textZhHtml: 我怀疑这是反捏造训练的标志。

children.author: lukaslalinsky
children.textRawHtml:
That was their headline for Opus 4.8, I guess the invested into some post-training to get them to write this, and I like it, it's a great way to identify AI posts from Claude.

https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-opus-4-8
children.textZhHtml:
这是他们的 Opus 4.8 标题，我想他们投入了一些后期培训才能写出这篇文章，我喜欢它，这是识别 Claude 的 AI 帖子的好方法。

https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-opus-4-8

children.author: jannyfer
children.textRawHtml:
Yup, and I had seen it in the Opus 4.5 soul document as well: https://gist.github.com/Richard-Weiss/efe157692991535403bd7e...

In the future, I expect different models from different firms behaving differently will become as obviously normal as different humans behaving differently. Those of us that have used agents a lot have already noticed this, but the general public still seems to consider AI to be plug-and-play.

The Opus 4.8 infatuation with being honest genuinely drove me nuts (honest take). The constant need for me to decipher and decide on something after its final message was tiring too.

GPT-5.6-Sol is refreshing that its replies start with "understood." instead of flattering me for my steering prompt. Now ... after a few weeks, will I get suck of "understood"? I dunno :)
children.textZhHtml:
是的，我也在 Opus 4.5 灵魂文档中看到了它：https://gist.github.com/Richard-Weiss/efe157692991535403bd7e...

将来，我预计不同公司的不同模型表现不同将变得像不同的人表现不同一样明显正常。我们这些经常使用代理的人已经注意到了这一点，但公众似乎仍然认为人工智能是即插即用的。

Opus 4.8 对诚实的迷恋确实让我发疯（诚实地说）。在收到最终消息后，我不断地需要破译并做出决定，这也很累人。

GPT-5.6-Sol 令人耳目一新，它的回复以“理解”开头。而不是因为我的转向提示而恭维我。现在……几周后，我会因为“理解”而感到厌烦吗？我不知道:)

children.author: LoganDark
children.textRawHtml: It's as if it's sucking up to me or apologizing at every turn, even when it's done exactly what I've asked. Seems related to sycophancy, but different? (i.e. rather than unconditionally affirming the user it's unconditionally expecting to disappoint)
children.textZhHtml: 就好像它每次都在拍我的马屁或道歉，即使它完全按照我的要求做了。看似与阿谀奉承有关，但又有所不同？ （即，与其无条件肯定用户，不如无条件期待让用户失望）

children.author: culi
children.textRawHtml: Claude is listed as a contributor right there

children.author: rhdunn
children.textZhHtml: 这不是妖精，而是诚实的！

comments.author: walrus01
comments.textRawHtml:
My main question is whether when put into practical use, this can be measured in tokens/second, or more like 1 token per minute... I have seen locally hosted LLM that are as slow as 1 tok/second still be very useful if you give it a project to do something overnight and metaphorically walk away from it, check back with what it has done in 6 or 8 hours.

0.05 to 0.1 tok/s on the other hand, as reported in the URL for the lowest class of hardware, isn't really usable for much.

edit: I think this is a fantastic project in general concept, and look forward to seeing more efforts towards the general idea of being able to run a 350B to 900B size model locally, even if as slow as 1 tok/s, on hardware that ordinary people can afford. Anything along the general concept of "we have fast read NVME SSD storage, we have a big ass model on local disk, we'll read it at 11GB/tok as we need it, not try to load the whole thing".
comments.textZhHtml:
我的主要问题是，在投入实际使用时，这是否可以以令牌/秒来衡量，或者更像是每分钟 1 个令牌...我见过 慢至 1 tok/秒的本地托管 LLM 仍然非常有用，如果你给它一个项目，让它在一夜之间做某件事，然后隐喻地离开它，检查一下它在 6 或 8 小时内所做的事情。

0.05 到 0.1另一方面，正如 URL 中针对最低级别硬件所报告的那样，tok/s 并没有多大用处。

编辑：我认为这在总体概念上是一个很棒的项目，并期待看到更多的努力实现能够在普通人买得起的硬件上本地运行 350B 到 900B 大小的模型，即使速度慢至 1 tok/s。任何遵循“我们有快速读取 NVME SSD 存储，我们在本地磁盘上有一个大屁股模型，我们将在需要时以 11GB/tok 读取它，而不是尝试加载整个东西”的一般概念的任何内容。

children.author: JumpCrisscross
children.textRawHtml:
The funny thing is Claude Cowork has taught me to be patient with response timelines. I’m now figuring I’ll be running locally no later than 2028.

(I want to spend no more than $10k. And I want to run a model comparable to today’s SOTA.)
children.textZhHtml:
有趣的是 Claude Cowork 教会我对响应时间表要有耐心。我现在预计，我将在 2028 年之前在本地运行。

（我希望花费不超过 1 万美元。而且我希望运行一个与今天的 SOTA 相当的模型。）

children.author: walrus01
children.textRawHtml:
For 10k you can buy a used dual socket Intel or amd based rackmount server with a terabyte of ram, and run models on cpu only at a reasonable speed. Same server would have been 4-5k a couple years ago before ram price rise.

Or buy one on eBay with 512GB that has half its slots populated and then buy the matching 512GB kit to add.
children.textZhHtml:
只需 10k，您就可以购买一台二手双路 Intel 或 AMD 机架式服务器，具有 TB 内存，并且仅以合理的速度在 CPU 上运行模型。几年前，在内存价格上涨之前，同一台服务器的价格可能是 4-5k。

或者在 eBay 上购买一台 512GB 的服务器，其中一半的插槽已填充，然后购买配套的 512GB 套件进行添加。
